quote: We might be reaching a plateau in terms of sheer capability. Nobody has been able to beat it decisively.
claim: AI capabilities may be reaching a plateau at the GPT-4 level, with no competitor decisively surpassing it after one year.

quote: Places like Google and Anthropic have put in a lot of money trying. None succeeded; instead there tentatively seems to be convergence at GPT-4 levels.
claim: Google and Anthropic have spent heavily but converged at GPT-4-level performance rather than surpassing it.

quote: putting LLMs into production is hard; most work to date has been preliminary. Companies are starting to temper their expectations. Many initial expectations were unrealistic.
claim: Most enterprise LLM deployments have been preliminary rather than production-level, and initial corporate expectations for LLMs were unrealistic.

quote: Bad actors, who may have lower standards for reliability, appear to be using them for cybercrime and disinformation.
claim: Bad actors are using LLMs for cybercrime and disinformation because they have lower reliability standards than legitimate users.

quote: Some core problems have lingered, particularly hallucinations, instability and occasional nonsensical errors.
claim: Core LLM problems including hallucinations, instability, and nonsensical errors remain unsolved after one year of GPT-4.

quote: OpenAI was here first, but there's not a huge moat; many other players appear to be catching up. We can expect price wars.
claim: OpenAI has no significant competitive moat in LLMs, and price wars among LLM providers are expected.

quote: Profitability remains in question. Big models cost big $, and getting standout results might cost huge $; licensing and legal costs may be substantial.
claim: The profitability of large language models remains questionable due to high training, operating, licensing, and legal costs.

trigger: OpenAI VP Anna Makanju remarks at Columbia
quote: the inherent unreliability in these systems is deeply worrisome
claim: The inherent unreliability of LLM-based AI systems is a serious concern that undermines their positive applications.

quote: If lots of code gets written, fast, but that code is riddled with security problems, the net advantage on the positive side of the ledger may be less than anticipated.
claim: AI-generated code may produce a net negative due to security vulnerabilities, offsetting productivity gains.

quote: I am increasingly worried that the big tech companies are—in conjunction with a Congress that has not yet passed any substantive AI legislation—successfully architecting a grossly asymmetric world, in which the profits from the positive side flow to them, and the costs of the negative side are borne by everyone else.
claim: Big tech companies are structuring the AI ecosystem so that they capture the profits while society bears the costs of AI harms.

quote: If the 2024 election flips based on GenAI-created disinformation, there will be a huge cost to society, but it is unlikely that the GenAI companies will be held responsible in any serious way.
claim: If GenAI-created disinformation influences the 2024 election, AI companies are unlikely to be held legally responsible.

quote: poorly-written software, authored in part by GenAI, could spread malware leading to a massive security incident that dwarfs WannaCry
claim: GenAI-assisted code could lead to a massive security incident worse than WannaCry due to poor code quality.

quote: A $100B LLM is still an LLM. Driverless cars were crippled by the endless long tail of outliers; LLMs may well pattern similarly, working in many cases but often troubled by the unexpected, even when scaled.
claim: A $100B LLM will still suffer from the same fundamental limitations as current LLMs, particularly failure on outlier cases, regardless of scale.

quote: Reasoning and planning in unexpected scenarios are still likely to be flawed.
claim: LLM reasoning and planning in unexpected scenarios will remain flawed even at $100B scale.

quote: Even if a $100B LLM worked noticeably better than a $1B LLM, the economics for that investment are problematic.
claim: The economics of a $100B LLM are problematic because diminishing returns mean it would only be marginally better than far cheaper models on most tasks.

quote: Presumably the operating idea here is that (a) $100B will equal AGI and (b) that customers will pay literally anything for AGI. I doubt either premise is correct.
claim: Spending $100B on an LLM will not produce AGI, and customers would not pay unlimited fees for AGI even if it existed.

quote: If they are wrong, the net effect will be to delay AGI, because their project will siphon away talent from other approaches – just as it already has, but more so.
claim: The $100B Stargate project will delay AGI by siphoning talent away from alternative approaches to AI.

trigger: Wall Street Journal report on GenAI spending vs revenue
quote: $50B in, $3B out. That's obviously not sustainable.
claim: The generative AI industry spending $50B while generating only $3B in revenue is not a sustainable business model.

quote: If hallucinations were brought down to human expert levels by the end of 2024 I would be truly astonished. They are inherent to how the system makes predictions, and to the fact that LLMs themselves don't have the wherewithal to look things up and reason over them.
claim: LLM hallucinations will not be reduced to human expert levels by end of 2024 because they are inherent to how LLMs make predictions.

quote: The entire industry is based on hype, and on the specific hope that the kinds of problems we saw again and again with GPT-2, GPT-3, and GPT-4, not to mention Claude, Grok, Gemini, LLaMA, and so on, are on the verge of being solved — even as the reality is that all the big players are converging on systems that are roughly equivalent to GPT-4, all flawed in essentially the same ways.
claim: The GenAI industry is based on hype rather than substance, with all major models converging at GPT-4-level performance and sharing the same fundamental flaws.

quote: I am not confident Generative AI as currently envisioned will ever come together.
claim: Generative AI as currently envisioned may never achieve the reliability and capability needed to justify its costs.

quote: With everyone building similar models, there is no moat, hence lots of competition on price
claim: There is no competitive moat in LLMs because all major companies are building similar models, leading to price competition.

quote: current-sized models, already expensive, aren't reliable enough for many jobs
claim: Current LLMs are too unreliable for many production use cases while already being expensive to operate.

quote: So far most uses of LLMs have been for testing, not production-level systems.
claim: Most enterprise LLM deployments as of early 2024 are testing/experimental rather than production systems.

quote: the closest thing to a killer app is probably coding assistance, but the income there isn't massive enough to cover costs of the chips, legal etc.
claim: Coding assistance is the closest thing to a killer app for LLMs, but it does not generate enough revenue to cover infrastructure and legal costs.

quote: The initially-celebrated use case of "generative search" isn't reliable enough (and doesn't yet have a clear business model).
claim: Generative AI-powered search is not reliable enough for production use and lacks a viable business model.

quote: GPT-5 may be more satisfying but is likely even more expensive to train and operate.
claim: GPT-5 will likely be even more expensive to train and operate than GPT-4, compounding the profitability problem.

quote: If nobody (OpenAI, Google, or anyone else) releases a true quantum leap by the end of 2024, substantially addressing key issues around reliability, hallucination, data leakage, and security, the bubble may start to pop by this time next year.
claim: If no company releases a transformative AI model addressing reliability, hallucination, and security by end of 2024, the GenAI bubble may start popping by early 2025.

quote: GenAI will still exist, and find some use, but valuations and excitement may dissipate, reemerging only once genuine advances are made.
claim: Generative AI will persist but at reduced valuations and excitement levels until genuine technical advances are made.

quote: Nobody knows exactly how LLMs work, and that means that nobody can ever issue strong guarantees around them.
claim: It is impossible to provide strong security guarantees for LLMs because nobody fully understands how they work internally.

quote: Rule 1 of cybersecurity is to keep your attack surface small; in LLMs the attack surface appears to be infinite. That can't be good.
claim: LLMs have an effectively infinite attack surface, making them fundamentally insecure.

quote: Secure, trustworthy AI could be a great thing, but LLMs will never be that.
claim: LLMs will never be secure and trustworthy AI systems.

quote: If we don't get a handle on this problem, fast, nobody will believe anything.
claim: Without swift action against AI-generated misinformation, public trust in all information will collapse.

quote: fast, cheap, automated misinformation left unchecked threatens to undermine trust — and democracy itself.
claim: Unchecked AI-generated misinformation threatens to undermine both public trust and democratic institutions.

quote: we should require all AI-generated content to be labeled
claim: All AI-generated content should be required by law to be labeled as such.

trigger: New preprint on data and scaling (arXiv:2404.04125)
quote: Neural networks (at least in the configurations that have been dominant over the last three decades) have trouble generalizing beyond the multidimensional space that surrounds their training examples.
claim: Neural networks in their dominant configurations cannot reliably generalize beyond the space of their training examples.

trigger: New preprint on data and scaling (arXiv:2404.04125)
quote: That limits their ability to reason and plan reliably. It also drives their greediness with data
claim: Neural networks' inability to generalize beyond training data limits their reasoning and planning ability and drives their insatiable need for more data.

trigger: New preprint on data and scaling (arXiv:2404.04125)
quote: This is why driverless cars are still just demos, and why LLMs will never be reliable.
claim: LLMs will never be reliable because neural networks cannot generalize beyond their training distribution.

trigger: New preprint on data and scaling (arXiv:2404.04125)
quote: Sooner or later, the exponential greed for data will exceed what is available.
claim: The exponentially growing data requirements of LLMs will eventually exceed available training data.

trigger: New preprint on data and scaling (arXiv:2404.04125)
quote: To get to AGI, we need alternative approaches that can generalize better beyond the data on which they have been trained.
claim: Achieving AGI requires fundamentally different approaches from current neural networks that can generalize beyond training data.

trigger: NYT report on AI companies cutting ethical corners for data
quote: the brittleness (a term for unreliability); the greed for data that is causing the desperation that the Times described; the opacity (uninterpretability) that makes debugging, fairness, and engineering so hard; and the shallowness, meaning that generalizations are never complete.
claim: The four fundamental problems of deep learning identified in 2018 -- brittleness, data greed, opacity, and shallowness -- are exactly what is ailing GenAI in 2024.

trigger: NYT report on AI companies cutting ethical corners for data
quote: an intellectual monoculture — with no alternative approach that is nearly as well-funded
claim: AI research has become an intellectual monoculture focused on LLMs, with no alternative approach receiving comparable funding.

trigger: NYT report on AI companies cutting ethical corners for data
quote: a pile of unreliable systems that are unlikely to live up to the hype
claim: Current generative AI systems are unreliable and unlikely to live up to the hype surrounding them.

trigger: NYT report on AI companies cutting ethical corners for data
quote: We must invest heavily in alternative approaches and stop funding a losing hand to the infinite degree, crowding everything out.
claim: The AI field should invest heavily in alternative approaches to LLMs rather than continuing to pour resources into scaling current architectures.

trigger: Elon Musk AGI prediction on X Space
quote: I don't buy the predictions for the next year
claim: Musk's prediction that AI will be smarter than any individual human by end of 2025 is wrong.

quote: some late stage venture capital firms are sitting out the current GenAI rush
claim: Late-stage venture capital firms are beginning to avoid investing in the GenAI rush, signaling the bubble may not last.

quote: I could be persuaded that on some measures there was a doubling of capabilities for some set of months in 2020-2023, but I don't see that case at all for the last 13 months.
claim: LLM capabilities have not doubled in the 13 months since GPT-4's release, contrary to claims of continued exponential improvement.

quote: Instead, I see numerous signs that we have reached a period of diminishing returns.
claim: LLM development has entered a period of diminishing returns as of early 2024.

quote: Mollick's claim that "the rate of improvement in Large Language models show capabilities doubling every 5 to 14 months" is no longer holding true.
claim: The claim that LLM capabilities double every 5-14 months is no longer supported by recent evidence.

quote: The wall that I once warned about, in 2022, may finally be approaching.
claim: LLMs are approaching a capability wall or ceiling that limits further improvement.

quote: If we really have changed regimes, from rapid progress to diminishing returns, and hallucinations and stupid errors do linger, LLMs may never be ready for prime time.
claim: If LLM progress has shifted to diminishing returns, LLMs may never be reliable enough for production use.

quote: In the most extreme case, OpenAI's $86 billion valuation could look in hindsight like a WeWork moment for AI.
claim: OpenAI's $86 billion valuation may prove to be as overinflated as WeWork's was.

quote: If enthusiasm for GenAI dwindles and market valuations plummet, AI won't disappear, and LLMs won't disappear; they will still have their place as tools for statistical approximation.
claim: Even if the GenAI bubble bursts, LLMs will persist as useful tools for statistical approximation but at a lower scale than currently envisioned.

quote: Reliable, trustworthy AI is surely achievable, but we may need to go back to the drawing board to get there.
claim: Achieving reliable and trustworthy AI will require fundamentally new approaches beyond current LLM architectures.

quote: there about 5 - 7 recent models are on a par with GPT-4, but none is clearly and decisively ahead
claim: As of April 2024, 5-7 recent LLMs match GPT-4's level but none decisively surpasses it, suggesting convergence.

quote: Both damning and correct. People who believe in LLMs have played themselves.
claim: People who attribute deep intelligence to LLMs are being deceived by a mechanism analogous to a psychic's con -- the LLMentalist Effect.

quote: the cognitive capacities of LLMs are severely limited
claim: The cognitive capacities of LLMs are severely limited despite their impressive surface-level fluency.

quote: Right now there are likely hundreds, maybe thousands or even millions of such things.
claim: There are hundreds to millions of intellectual tasks that ordinary humans can do that AI cannot as of 2024.

quote: Reliably drive a car in a novel location that they haven't previously encountered, even in the face of unusual circumstances like hand-lettered signs, without the assistance of other humans.
claim: AI will not be able to reliably drive a car in novel locations with unusual circumstances by end of 2025.

quote: Learn and master the basics of almost any new video game within a few minutes or hours, and solve original puzzles in the alternate world of that video game.
claim: AI will not be able to master arbitrary new video games within minutes or hours by end of 2025.

quote: Write engaging biographies and obituaries without obvious hallucinations that aren't grounded in reliable sources.
claim: AI will not be able to write biographies and obituaries without hallucinations by end of 2025.

quote: Write cogent, persuasive legal briefs without hallucinating any cases
claim: AI will not be able to write legal briefs without hallucinating case citations by end of 2025.

quote: Write Pulitzer-caliber books, fiction and non-fiction
claim: AI will not be able to write Pulitzer-caliber books by end of 2025.

quote: Come up with paradigm-shifting, Nobel-caliber scientific discoveries.
claim: AI will not produce Nobel-caliber paradigm-shifting scientific discoveries by end of 2025.

quote: All of the above seem outside the bounds of current AI. None seem to me to be within immediate reach.
claim: A wide range of tasks from physical navigation to creative writing to scientific discovery remain outside the reach of current and near-term AI.

quote: they will not themselves, anytime soon, be virtual scientists
claim: LLMs will not function as virtual scientists capable of independent scientific discovery anytime soon.

quote: the multimodal stuff still is very buggy, having troubles with basics like the English alphabet
claim: Multimodal AI capabilities remain very buggy, struggling with basic tasks like rendering the English alphabet correctly.

quote: Yejin Choi worried that LLMs were not reliable. They still aren't. She was right.
claim: LLMs remain unreliable as of April 2024, confirming warnings from researchers like Yejin Choi.

quote: I warned that LLMs would be used by bad actors, and they increasingly are.
claim: LLMs are increasingly being used by bad actors for harmful purposes as predicted.

quote: hallucinations (e.g., on basic math) were again the key liability. You can't teach if your students don't know whether or not to believe you.
claim: LLM hallucinations are a fundamental barrier to using AI for education because students cannot trust unreliable outputs.

quote: I continue to have doubts about whether LLMs are really the right tool for the next step of the job
claim: LLMs may not be the right technology for advancing educational tools like Khan Academy.

quote: no company yet had A.I. technology that was sophisticated enough to make a virtual assistant answer questions reliably
claim: No company as of April 2024 has AI technology sophisticated enough to make a virtual assistant reliably answer questions.

quote: It's almost like a broken watch being right twice a day. It's right some of the time, but you don't know which part of the time, and that greatly diminishes its value.
claim: AI virtual assistants are unreliable in a way that greatly diminishes their value -- users cannot predict when they will be correct.

quote: I don't think we are any closer to a machine with the wherewithal or the motivation to annihilate the human species than we were a year ago.
claim: AI has not moved meaningfully closer to posing an existential risk to humanity between 2023 and 2024.

quote: Bugs in LLMs have stymied Khanmigo and the AI pin. Adding visual data to LLMs hasn't solved hallucinations.
claim: Adding multimodal capabilities to LLMs has not solved the hallucination problem.

trigger: TED 2024 talks by Suleyman, Khosla, Li
quote: I don't see how we get to a positive AI future if we sweep all the problems under the rug.
claim: Achieving a positive AI future requires honestly confronting current AI problems rather than ignoring them.

trigger: TED 2024 talks by Suleyman, Khosla, Li
quote: I think Khosla's timing (all this by 2049) might be off, perhaps by decades
claim: Vinod Khosla's predictions about AI transforming medicine, agriculture, and infrastructure by 2049 may be off by decades.

trigger: TED 2024 talks by Suleyman, Khosla, Li
quote: current AI needs better spatial intelligence
claim: Current AI systems need fundamentally better spatial intelligence to achieve real-world physical capabilities.

trigger: TED 2024 talks by Suleyman, Khosla, Li
quote: We won't get to a billion personal robots if they are as dodgy as driverless cars, frequently working, yet stymied often enough by outliers that we can't fully count on them.
claim: Achieving widespread personal robotics requires solving the reliability problem that plagues driverless cars -- robots that fail on outlier cases cannot be trusted.

trigger: TED 2024 talks by Suleyman, Khosla, Li
quote: A billion robots with a superficial understanding of the physical world would be a recipe for disaster.
claim: Deploying robots at scale with only superficial physical world understanding would be dangerous.

trigger: TED 2024 talks by Suleyman, Khosla, Li
quote: Dreamers who promise far more than they are likely to deliver are getting almost all the money, and (at least in AI) they are getting so much money for a path that it is unlikely to work that they are leaving too little oxygen for the exploration of new ideas that might work.
claim: Venture capital is disproportionately funding AI approaches that are unlikely to work, starving alternative approaches of resources.

trigger: TED 2024 talks by Suleyman, Khosla, Li
quote: I doubt that anyone with a short leash can achieve the breakthroughs we will need, if we are to get to the optimistic future so many of us would like to see.
claim: Achieving AI breakthroughs requires long-term research funding, not the typical 3-year VC timeline for product-market fit.

trigger: TED 2024 talks by Suleyman, Khosla, Li
quote: Cars would never have blossomed without streetlights and traffic laws, and too many people would have died without seatbelts.
claim: AI regulation is necessary for AI to flourish, just as traffic laws were necessary for automobiles to succeed.

trigger: TED 2024 talks by Suleyman, Khosla, Li
quote: We don't want AI to wind up stigmatized like nuclear energy, a good solution that was rolled out with insufficient care and permanently tarnished.
claim: Without proper regulation and care, AI risks becoming permanently stigmatized like nuclear energy.

quote: Like so many recent models, the new model approached GPT-4 but didn't exceed it, consistent (but not proof of) my ongoing conjecture of diminishing returns.
claim: Meta's LLAMA-3 approached but did not exceed GPT-4, providing further evidence for the hypothesis of diminishing returns in LLM scaling.

quote: Whatever tiny moat there was between the companies will further diminish. LLMs are well on their way to becoming a commodity; price wars will be intense.
claim: LLMs are becoming commoditized, which will erode competitive advantages and trigger intense price wars among providers.

trigger: Meta LLAMA-3 release and stock market decline
quote: 2024 could be remembered as the high point in a kind of tulip-mania.
claim: 2024 may mark the peak of a tulip-mania-like bubble in AI investment.

quote: all the major AI companies are spending billions producing almost exactly the same results using almost exactly the same data using almost exactly the same technology — all flawed in almost exactly the same ways
claim: All major AI companies are producing nearly identical LLM results with the same technology and data, all sharing the same fundamental flaws -- a dangerous groupthink.

quote: hallucinations are absolutely core to how LLMs work, and that we need new approaches based on different ideas.
claim: Hallucinations are a fundamental feature of how LLMs work, not a fixable bug, and solving the problem requires fundamentally new approaches.
